chapters.title: Who are the cynics?
chapters.text:
They trash social norms, the politics of their countries, the naive moves of their friends, and pretty much anything most people value. They act more capable than everyone else, or at least they want to. They refuse to let anyone shift their view, because they trust only themselves. Meet the cynics.

Pessimism and disappointment are not a free pass to handle problems passively.

The ancient Greek thinker Diogenes of Athens was the first well-known cynic. He refused to tie himself to any political camp or social class so he could stand on equal footing with anyone.

He also left his home and everyday goods behind to learn the true core of life through simple living. And, most importantly, he never avoided people. On the contrary, he always looked for reasons to talk with strangers, sometimes even being a pest, but only to get at their character and way of thinking.

His principles and values were very different from those of the cynics who came after him. Wondering how cynics turned into what they are now, and what can pull them out? Let us find out.

chapters.title: Move or rust
chapters.text:
There is a popular myth that links a person’s intelligence to the choice of cynicism as a guiding value.

Knowledge gives us a sense of control and the power to predict outcomes, and it shapes how we plan our lives. What do people do when they cannot find answers or do not like what they hear? They often hunt for other sources to make their views feel universal. Even if those data do not match reality, they suit the cynics because they line up with their worldview and feed it.

We also tend to cling to decisions and reactions that have worked for us before and turn them into habits. It feels great to know how to answer a familiar question or react to a situation we have been in before, does it not?

There is a big catch in this seemingly winning position. Information in the modern world updates very fast, and skills become outdated just as fast. People lose when they think they will stay ahead by repeating the same choices.

Test your assumptions by listening to opinions that differ from your own.

Let us look quickly at Thomas’s life. Until the age of twelve, his life felt like a fairy tale, with yearly family trips to the seaside and the best gifts at Christmas. One day, his dad packed his biggest suitcase and left their house. From then on, his presence in Thomas’s life shrank to video calls and birthday money transfers.

Thomas grew up, and his disappointment in the people closest to him became cynicism as a shield. In his view, neither work nor personal bonds were worth the effort, because sooner or later he would face betrayal or the collapse of his hopes.

Sadly, Thomas’s story is not unique. We could make a long list of life situations that feed the rise of cynics. Let us focus instead on the fact that people grow more open to other views and revise their principles when they feel safe and ready to trust.

chapters.title: Do you trust me?
chapters.text:
Since 1972, the General Social Survey has asked US citizens about their trust in other people and institutions. Tracking the answers over fifty years, they found that during that time, 29% fewer people trusted the President, 21% fewer trusted the media, and 35% fewer trusted Congress.

Let us look at a special social construct, trust. It is hard to earn, and people fear losing it. Sometimes, they cannot explain why they trust strangers and rumors, while keeping their distance from friends and verified sources.

Trust is a social gamble, and cynics think it is for suckers, as Jamil Zaki once wrote.

Even though plenty of trouble has happened since then, try to answer this. Have you started to trust your country’s government more since COVID-19?

Based on polls, most US citizens would answer no. At the same time, people in South Korea would not agree. Their government was honest about the news, including the facts about COVID that were surfacing and the questions still open at the time. That transparency shaped trust, led to high vaccination rates, and saved many lives.

The first necessary step in tough times is to trust others.

Cynics say that everyone wants to deceive and harm each other, and only naive people trust others in such an unreliable world. The fact remains. The more we trust people and open our hearts, the higher the chance that we will get help and support when we need it, and we will not feel abandoned or misunderstood by the people around us.

Trust is about courage, not weakness. It does not mean we should accept and agree with everything that happens around us. Trusting and engaging with people helps us see what matters to us, rather than rejecting everything on sight the way cynics do.

chapters.title: Prices on the social marketplace
chapters.text:
Social media influencers, psychotherapists, and the general push toward individualism all push people to focus on themselves and think in terms of me, putting everyone else second. The environment still shapes each of us just as much as parents do.

It is a paradox. Social networks and the digital world have made it easier to communicate, and at the same time, they have pulled people apart and made understanding harder. The online format pushes us to chase quantitative approval in the form of likes and comments. The platforms that could lift us up turn into fake communities built on unhealthy competition or the urge to give up and turn against everyone.

As members of the same society, we shape each other through our actions and through our silence. It depends on what we want more, to live in a cold environment where people look at each other with distrust and stay under constant stress, or in a safe community where we can count on each other.

Counting the good deeds you do for others and expecting something back kills real kindness.

Of course, our behavior depends on where we are and what role we play. Not everyone can hint to their boss or professor that they need to check the level of cynicism in their blood. But it is within everyone’s power to build circles of trust in their football clubs, university classes, or volunteer groups in the neighborhood.

If you have people you help, give your time to, or share resources with without thinking about what they owe you, you are on the right track. Cynicism is afraid of strong communities.

After World War II, communist ministries in the newly created East Germany hired locals to report on people’s attitudes toward the authorities. Citizens stopped feeling safe at work, in shops, and even around friends. The result was a low level of trust that stayed low even after the fall of the Berlin Wall.

chapters.title: Things are going better than we think
chapters.text:
For thirty years, the American polling firm Gallup has run surveys to gauge people’s views on the level of crime in the country. In 1989, the answers lined up closely with the actual numbers. By 2020, they had drifted far apart. Do you think people were too optimistic or too pessimistic?

According to the Federal Bureau of Investigation, the number of crimes dropped by 50% over those years, while poll respondents expected the numbers to keep getting worse every year.

Here is another telling case where cynical assumptions did not hold up. The Grameen Bank is a Bangladeshi group that gives out loans with no collateral. Professor Muhammad Yunus started the project after meeting craftspeople from a village hit hard by famine in the 1970s.

Did his colleagues warn him that no one would pay him back? Of course. And what was the share of people who did pay him back? 99%.

In 2006, Professor Muhammad Yunus and his organization won the Nobel Peace Prize for proving the value of looking for the good in people and trusting one another.

It is easy to be a cynic. Building new chances for yourself and others is much harder.

Bad news and misfortune hit harder and stick in memory more than happy events. News and media programs focus on incidents, making them more visible in the information space. Many assumptions lean toward a situation getting worse rather than better.

Those automatic thoughts play into the hands of cynicism, which proclaims selfishness, greed, and betrayal as the main rules of humanity. So we have to learn more about the wins of good over evil.

That is exactly the goal the artist David Byrne set for himself when he created Reasons to Be Cheerful. This outlet publishes articles about projects that study and solve world problems. It also invites readers to join in creating positive change around them. Who knows, maybe this is the resource that will save our cynics.

chapters.title: The wind of change
chapters.text:
Finally, there is an argument most cynics are not ready for. We live in communities and shape each other, and we also change others and ourselves.

History has plenty of evidence for this. Before becoming the first president of the independent Czech Republic, Václav Havel wrote plays and essays that criticized the communist regime, and he joined the Prague Spring in 1968. The government started watching him closely after Havel co-founded the civil rights group Charter 77. The secret police eventually gathered enough evidence to imprison him for five years.

Havel saw too many positive effects of his work to give up. He kept writing even in prison and stayed in touch with people who shared his views. Six years after his release, in November 1989, he led the Velvet Revolution, which toppled the communist government.

New knowledge and changes expand the field of our influence and lower the level of mistrust in the world.

We do not have to become presidents to see change happen. It can show up while chatting with strangers, talking about a movie with family, or reading a book a friend recommended. The steps can be so small that the coming change is hard to notice, but it is there in what we know and in our new values and habits.

Biologically speaking, the only thing human beings cannot do is not change, as Jamil Zaki once wrote.

Cynics are repeatedly disappointed in how the world around them behaves, and they judge everyone else the same way, not giving others a chance to prove them wrong.

Over time, though, they may notice that their surroundings have stopped following the usual patterns and meeting their expectations. Now that we know how steady change is, we can guess that cynics will follow that path too.

Of course, they can still call it all a mistake and shut themselves away in a world where it is not worth expecting anything good from other people. But they will also have evidence that their environment can get better. With that, they will be nudged to change with everyone else.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
If there were a court of justice issuing a general verdict on all of humanity, the cynics would happily take the role of the accusers, with an endless list of people’s flaws.

The truth is, that is an easy task, because the world is full of problems and people keep disappointing each other. What is far harder and more responsible is to keep finding the courage to trust others, build community, and shape a better world together.

A few things you can try. Turn off your devices for a day, a week, or a month and look for connections and new information offline. Write down the names of five people you talk with most often. Think about why you put them first and what they can teach you. Map out your growth over the last ten years. What useful contacts did you make, what skills did you pick up, what habits did you drop?

Thanks for reading to the end.
